message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 19
Recent Green phase: Added processClaimStep with sumOver damages and DEDUCTIBLE_PER_DAMAGE = 100.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to next test after completion.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll start by reading the architecture notes and current implementation to evaluate refactoring opportunities.

content.content:
architecture-notes.md
eslint.config.mjs
example-mapping

node_modules

src

1	# Architecture Notes
2	
3	## After cycle 1
4	- Change: tightened `runScenario` return type from `{ results: unknown[] }` to `{ results: { premium: number }[] }`
5	- Rationale: Rule 2 (reveals intent) — documents the shape tests exercise; `premium: number` field-name contract is now visible at the signature
6	- Naming established: `runScenario(scenario) -> { results: { premium: number }[] }`; field name `premium` confirmed (per example-mapping contract)
7	
8	## After cycle 2
9	- Change: extracted magic numbers in `runScenario` to module-level named constants `FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE = 0.1` and `PROCESSING_FEE = 5`
10	- Rationale: Rule 2 (reveals intent) — these are global pricing-rule constants that more upcoming tests will reference; naming them centralises and documents intent at every use site. Rule 2 trumps APP (mass +5).
11	- Naming established: `FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE` (10% surcharge on first-insurance, applied to policy base), `PROCESSING_FEE` (flat 5 G added to every quote), `BASE_PREMIUM` (lookup table by item type); per-step local names `policyBase` (sum of item base premiums) and `firstInsurance` (surcharge amount, not boolean flag)
12	
13	## After cycle 3
14	- Change: extracted per-step pricing into a private module-level function `priceQuoteStep(step: QuoteStep) -> { premium: number }`; `runScenario` is now just `scenario.steps.map(priceQuoteStep)`
15	- Rationale: Rule 2 (reveals intent) — names the "price one quote step" concept that was previously anonymous inside `.map(...)`. Upcoming surcharges (curse, high-enchantment, loyalty, follow-up) have a clear home and won't bloat `runScenario`. Mass roughly neutral.
16	- Naming established: `priceQuoteStep(step)` is the canonical per-step pricing helper; future surcharges (curse, high-enchantment, loyalty discount, follow-up discount) should compose inside this function, not in `runScenario`
17	
18	## After cycle 4
19	- Change: extracted `itemBasePremium(item)` and `itemSurcharges(item)` helpers; named the `0.5` magic number as `CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE`; renamed local `curseSurcharge` to `itemSurchargeTotal` (now an accumulator for all per-item surcharges, not just curse).
20	- Rationale: Rule 3 (no duplication) — the `BASE_PREMIUM[item.type] ?? 0` lookup was duplicated across two reduces; now centralised in `itemBasePremium`. Rule 2 (reveals intent) — `itemSurcharges(item)` is the natural extension point for upcoming high-enchantment (per-item) surcharge.
21	- Naming established: `itemBasePremium(item) -> number` (single source of base-premium lookup, will absorb rune-block logic later); `itemSurcharges(item) -> number` (sum of all per-item surcharges — add high-enchantment here next); `CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.5`; the local accumulator name `itemSurchargeTotal` (NOT `curseSurcharge`) reflects that it sums all per-item surcharges.
22	
23	## After cycle 5
24	- Change: rewrote `itemSurcharges` from imperative `let surcharge = 0; if (…) surcharge += …` accumulator to two named expression bindings (`curseSurcharge`, `highEnchantmentSurcharge`) summed in the return; added constants `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.3` and `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD = 5`.
25	- Rationale: Rule 4 (fewest elements) — eliminated 3 assignments and a mutable `let`, reducing APP mass ~43 → ~26 (Δ -17). Rule 2 (reveals intent) — each surcharge component is now a named value at the call site, so future surcharges added inside `itemSurcharges` follow a clear pattern: `const xSurcharge = condition ? base * X_RATE : 0`.
26	- Naming established: pattern for new per-item surcharges is a named binding `<name>Surcharge = condition ? base * <NAME>_RATE : 0`; the return is a flat sum of these bindings (no mutable accumulator). Constants `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.3`, `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD = 5`.
27	
28	## After cycle 6
29	- Change: introduced loyalty-discount logic in cycle 6's green phase (`LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE = 0.2`, `LOYALTY_THRESHOLD_YEARS = 2`, applied to `policyBase` in `priceQuoteStep`); refactored by extracting a generic `sumOver<T>(xs, f)` helper and replacing the two duplicated `step.items.reduce((sum, x) => sum + f(x), 0)` call sites with `sumOver(step.items, itemBasePremium)` and `sumOver(step.items, itemSurcharges)`.
30	- Rationale: Rule 3 (no duplication) — the "sum f(x) over a collection" pattern was duplicated. Rule 2 (reveals intent) — `priceQuoteStep` now reads as a flat sequence of named pricing terms with no `reduce` noise; the per-step total mass dropped from ~44 to ~23. The helper will be reused for sum-of-payouts in upcoming claim tests.
31	- Naming established: `sumOver<T>(xs, f)` is the canonical "sum a function over a collection" helper — use it instead of inlining `xs.reduce((s, x) => s + f(x), 0)`. `LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE = 0.2`, `LOYALTY_THRESHOLD_YEARS = 2`; loyalty discount is applied to `policyBase` only (NOT to surcharges, fee, or first-insurance), inside `priceQuoteStep`.
32	
33	## After cycle 7
34	- Change: introduced follow-up discount (`FOLLOW_UP_DISCOUNT_RATE = 0.15`, new `isFollowUp: boolean` param to `priceQuoteStep`, `runScenario` passes `index > 0`) in cycle 7's green phase; refactored by extracting `Math.ceil(...)` at the premium return into a named helper `roundUpInFavorOfOffice(amount)` with a docstring naming the MHPCO domain rule.
35	- Rationale: Rule 2 (reveals intent) — the spec explicitly calls out "rounded UP in MHPCO's favor" as a domain rule (visible in pending tests like "rounded UP to 198 in MHPCO's favor"); naming it pins that domain meaning at the call site instead of leaving a bare `Math.ceil`. Also opens the symmetric home for `roundDownInFavorOfOffice` that upcoming claim-payout tests require ("payout rounded DOWN in MHPCO's favor: 350.5 → 350"). Mass ~+3 — Rule 2 trumps APP, consistent with cycles 2/6.
36	- Naming established: `roundUpInFavorOfOffice(amount)` for quote premiums; the symmetric `roundDownInFavorOfOffice(amount)` (using `Math.floor`) is the expected home for claim-payout rounding. `FOLLOW_UP_DISCOUNT_RATE = 0.15`; follow-up discount is applied to `policyBase` only (same scope as loyalty) inside `priceQuoteStep`. `isFollowUp: boolean` is computed by `runScenario` (`index > 0`) rather than inside `priceQuoteStep`, because positional context is only known at the scenario level — this asymmetry vs `yearsWithMHPCO` (customer field) is intentional and correct.
37	
38	## After cycle 8
39	- Change: added `rune: 25` to `BASE_PREMIUM` in cycle 8's green phase (satisfies "2 runes → 50 base" via uniform per-item lookup, no block logic yet); refactored by extracting `type QuoteResult = { premium: number }` and replacing the two inline `{ premium: number }` literals in `priceQuoteStep`'s and `runScenario`'s return types.
40	- Rationale: Rule 3 (no duplication) — the `{ premium: number }` shape was duplicated across two return-type annotations; now centralised. Rule 2 (reveals intent) — names the "priced quote result" concept; upcoming claim-payout tests will produce a parallel `ClaimResult = { payout: number }` shape, and having a sibling `QuoteResult` named type makes that distinction obvious. Did NOT split `BASE_PREMIUM` into items/components or extract a `RUNE_BLOCK_SIZE` constant — premature; only the 2-rune (no-block) case is currently a passing test. Rune-block logic enters in cycle 9 ("3 runes → 60 block base") and `itemBasePremium` is the established home for it (per cycle 4 notes).
41	- Naming established: `type QuoteResult = { premium: number }` is the canonical priced-quote-result shape — both `priceQuoteStep` and `runScenario.results[]` reference it; expect a sibling `type ClaimResult = { payout: number }` when claim-payout tests begin (cycle 13+). `BASE_PREMIUM` still mixes items and components (correct for now — distinction only matters once block-pricing exists).
42	
43	## After cycle 9
44	- Change: cycle 9's green phase added an inline `runeCount === 3 ? -15 : 0` adjustment in `priceQuoteStep` (NOT in `itemBasePremium` as cycle 4 had speculated — block pricing is about *groups*, not individual items, so it correctly lives at the step level). Refactored by extracting `RUNE_BLOCK_SIZE = 3`, `RUNE_BLOCK_PRICE = 60`, and deriving `RUNE_BLOCK_ADJUSTMENT = RUNE_BLOCK_PRICE - RUNE_BLOCK_SIZE * BASE_PREMIUM.rune` (= -15). Replaced the two magic numbers in `priceQuoteStep` with these names.
45	- Rationale: Rule 2 (reveals intent) — the derivation makes the domain rule explicit ("3 runes sold as a block of 60 G instead of raw 75 G") rather than leaving readers to reverse-engineer why -15 matters. Mass roughly neutral at the call site; +~10 at module scope — Rule 2 trumps APP, consistent with cycles 2/6/7. Did NOT yet generalise to "any component type in blocks of size N" — only rune is a component; pending tests for moonstone blocks (cycles 12-13) will force that generalisation, and the right shape is currently uncertain (per-type block size? per-type block price?). Cycle 4's prediction that `itemBasePremium` would absorb block logic was wrong — block pricing depends on count-across-items, which is fundamentally a step-level concern.
46	- Naming established: `RUNE_BLOCK_SIZE = 3`, `RUNE_BLOCK_PRICE = 60`, derived `RUNE_BLOCK_ADJUSTMENT`; the local `runeBlockAdjustment` binding in `priceQuoteStep` is the canonical home for block-pricing corrections (additive adjustment to `policyBase`). When moonstone-block tests arrive (cycle 12+), expect this to generalise to a `componentBlockAdjustment(step.items)` helper or a per-type lookup like `COMPONENT_BLOCKS: Record<string, { size: number; price: number }>` — pick the shape that the failing test most cheaply demands at that time.
47	
48	## After cycle 10
49	- No refactoring (justified): cycle 10's green added a single line (`moonstone: 25`) to the existing `BASE_PREMIUM` lookup, which is the established home for per-type prices (cycle 4). Naming evaluation: `BASE_PREMIUM` still accurately describes its role; no rename warranted. Rule 3: `rune`/`moonstone` both being 25 G is coincidental (spec-driven), not conceptual duplication — extracting a `COMPONENT_BASE_PRICE = 25` would artificially couple them. Rule 2: deliberately did NOT generalise to a `componentBlockAdjustment` helper or `COMPONENT_BLOCKS` lookup — the moonstone-block test ("3 moonstones → block") is still `it.todo`; the right shape is clearer once that failing test exists (cycle 11). Premature generalisation would violate test-driven design, consistent with cycle 8 ("Did NOT split BASE_PREMIUM into items/components") and cycle 9 ("Did NOT yet generalise to any component type in blocks").
50	
51	## After cycle 11
52	- Change: cycle 11's green generalised block pricing to all components via inline `sumOver(COMPONENT_TYPES, type => ...)` inside `priceQuoteStep` (with `COMPONENT_TYPES = ["rune", "moonstone"]`, `COMPONENT_BLOCK_SIZE = 3`, `COMPONENT_BLOCK_PRICE = 60`, replacing cycle 9's `RUNE_BLOCK_*`). Refactored by (a) extracting `basePremium(type: string) -> number` as the single source of price-table lookup and (b) extracting the inline block-adjustment into a module-level `componentBlockAdjustment(items: QuoteItem[]) -> number` helper. `itemBasePremium` now delegates to `basePremium`; the block helper also calls `basePremium` instead of inlining `BASE_PREMIUM[type] ?? 0`.
53	- Rationale: Rule 3 (no duplication) — `BASE_PREMIUM[x] ?? 0` was inlined in two places (`itemBasePremium` and the cycle-9-style block computation); now centralised in `basePremium`. Rule 2 (reveals intent) — `priceQuoteStep`'s `policyBase` line is now a flat sum of two named terms (`sumOver(step.items, itemBasePremium) + componentBlockAdjustment(step.items)`) with no inline `sumOver(COMPONENT_TYPES, ...)` complexity; the block helper has a docstring naming the domain rule. Net mass at `priceQuoteStep` dropped ~18; module-level mass roughly neutral. Rule 2 trumps APP, consistent with cycles 2/6/7/9.
54	- Naming established: `basePremium(type: string) -> number` is the SINGLE source of price-table lookup — use it instead of `BASE_PREMIUM[x] ?? 0` anywhere new. `itemBasePremium(item)` is now a thin wrapper that delegates to `basePremium(item.type)`; both are kept because `itemBasePremium` matches the `sumOver(step.items, itemBasePremium)` signature. `componentBlockAdjustment(items: QuoteItem[]) -> number` is the canonical home for block-pricing corrections — when claim-payout tests arrive (cycle 13+) and need similar per-type aggregation, follow this `sumOver(COMPONENT_TYPES, type => ...)` pattern. `COMPONENT_TYPES`, `COMPONENT_BLOCK_SIZE`, `COMPONENT_BLOCK_PRICE` replace the rune-specific names from cycle 9.
55

content.text:
Now I have full context. Let me analyze cycle 12 (the just-completed claim payout test).

**Step 1: Naming Evaluation**

Current name: `processClaimStep`
Function purpose: computes payout for a claim incident — sums (damage.amount − DEDUCTIBLE_PER_DAMAGE) over damages.

The existing naming conventions are:
- `priceQuoteStep` for quote pricing (verb + noun)
- `processClaimStep` for claim processing

Comparison: `priceQuoteStep` uses a domain-specific verb ("price"). `processClaimStep` uses a generic verb ("process") that doesn't convey what it actually does — it computes a payout. By parallel construction, `payoutForClaimStep` would be more specific, but it loses verb form. Let me consider `computeClaimPayout` or `payClaimStep`.

Actually, looking at this domain (insurance), "process a claim" IS standard insurance terminology — it's the canonical verb for what an insurance office does with claims. The journal/example-mapping likely uses this language. I'll keep `processClaimStep` since "process" is the established domain verb for the claim handling concept, matching how the office terminology actually works.

**Step 2: Calculate Initial APP Mass for `processClaimStep`**

```typescript
function processClaimStep(step: ClaimStep): ClaimResult {
  const payout = sumOver(
    step.incident.damages,
    (damage) => damage.amount - DEDUCTIBLE_PER_DAMAGE,
  );
  return { payout };
}
```

- Constants: 0
- Bindings: 4 (step, payout, damage, plus DEDUCTIBLE_PER_DAMAGE binding usage) ≈ 4 = 4
- Invocations: 3 (sumOver, -, return object construction) ≈ 3 × 2 = 6
- Conditionals: 0
- Loops: 0 (sumOver is a function call, not a loop syntactic construct)
- Assignments: 0

Total: ~10

**Step 3: Apply Simple Design Rules**

**Rule 1 (Tests Pass):** Let me verify.

input.description: Run tests to verify green state

content.text:
All 19 tests passing.

**Rule 2 (Reveals Intent):**

Looking at `processClaimStep`:
- The inline arrow `(damage) => damage.amount - DEDUCTIBLE_PER_DAMAGE` is a per-damage payout calculation. The pattern across the codebase establishes named helpers like `itemBasePremium(item)` and `itemSurcharges(item)` that take a single domain entity and return a number, then are used with `sumOver`. 
- Extracting a `damagePayout(damage)` helper would follow the established pattern from cycles 4 and 6.
- Upcoming claim tests (high-enchantment 50% clause, dragon-material clause, per-damage deductible) will all add complexity to the per-damage calculation. Cycle 4 set the precedent of pre-extracting an extension point before complexity arrives, just like `itemSurcharges(item)`.

**Rule 3 (No Duplication):** No duplication currently.

**Rule 4 (Fewest Elements):** Code is already minimal — but Rule 2 trumps APP.

**Step 4: Implement Refactoring**

Extract `damagePayout(damage: DamageEntry) -> number` as the canonical per-damage payout helper, mirroring `itemBasePremium`/`itemSurcharges`. This establishes the natural home for upcoming 50%-clause and dragon-material logic (cycles 13+).

content.text:
Wait — I'm being premature. Cycles 8 and 10 explicitly warned against premature extraction. Right now only ONE claim test exists. The `damageReimbursement` helper has nothing to do — it's just `damage.amount`. That violates Rule 4 (fewest elements) and adds noise.

Let me reconsider. The minimal Rule-2 improvement that's justified RIGHT NOW: extract just `damagePayout(damage)` to give the per-damage payout concept a name, matching `itemBasePremium`/`itemSurcharges`. Skip the speculative `damageReimbursement` split — wait until a failing test forces it.

input.new_string:
## After cycle 12
- Change: cycle 12's green added `processClaimStep` with inline `sumOver(step.incident.damages, (damage) => damage.amount - DEDUCTIBLE_PER_DAMAGE)` and the `DEDUCTIBLE_PER_DAMAGE = 100` constant. Refactored by extracting the per-damage payout into a module-level helper `damagePayout(damage: DamageEntry) -> number`; `processClaimStep` is now `sumOver(step.incident.damages, damagePayout)`. Did NOT split out a `damageReimbursement(damage) = damage.amount` helper (would be a pointless wrapper around a single field access with only one passing test) — that split only earns its keep once the 50% high-enchantment clause or dragon-material full-reimbursement clause arrives.
- Rationale: Rule 2 (reveals intent) — `damagePayout` names the "payout for one damage entry" concept at the call site, matching the established `itemBasePremium(item)`/`itemSurcharges(item)` pattern (cycles 4, 6) that pairs with `sumOver`. This is the canonical extension point for upcoming clause logic (high-enchantment 50%, dragon-material full reimbursement, per-damage deductible variations across cycles 13-20). Net mass +~1 — Rule 2 trumps APP, consistent with cycles 2/6/7/9/11.
- Naming established: `damagePayout(damage: DamageEntry) -> number` is the canonical per-damage payout helper — upcoming high-enchantment 50% clause and dragon-material full-reimbursement clause should compose INSIDE this function, NOT in `processClaimStep`. Follows the same shape as `itemSurcharges(item)`: per-entity computation that pairs with `sumOver` at the step level. `DEDUCTIBLE_PER_DAMAGE = 100` is currently subtracted unconditionally inside `damagePayout`; if a future clause requires the deductible to be computed differently (e.g. computed before/after a percentage clause), restructure as `damageBaseReimbursement(damage) - DEDUCTIBLE_PER_DAMAGE` then. The symmetric `roundDownInFavorOfOffice(amount)` (mirror of `roundUpInFavorOfOffice` from cycle 7) is still expected for the "payout rounded DOWN to 350" test (cycle 20) — defer until that failing test exists.

## After cycle 11

content.text:
Refactor Phase Complete.

**Refactoring**: Extracted `damagePayout(damage: DamageEntry) -> number` module-level helper; `processClaimStep` is now `sumOver(step.incident.damages, damagePayout)`. This mirrors the established `itemBasePremium`/`itemSurcharges` pattern (cycles 4, 6) and pre-creates the natural composition home for upcoming high-enchantment 50% clause and dragon-material full-reimbursement clause (cycles 13+).

**Naming Evaluation**: Considered renaming `processClaimStep` but kept it — "process a claim" is the canonical insurance-domain verb, parallel to `priceQuoteStep`. Deliberately did NOT extract a premature `damageReimbursement(damage) = damage.amount` wrapper (would violate Rule 4 with only one passing test) — consistent with cycle 8/10 discipline against speculative generalisation.

**Mass Change**: ~10 → ~11 (Δ +1) — Rule 2 (reveals intent) trumps APP, consistent with cycles 2/6/7/9/11.

**Tests**: All 19 passing.

- `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-21_22-55-29_claim-office-lite-example-mapping_v4.2-shared-context_opus-4-7-portkey-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`
